entry.title: Control panels / alt interface
entry.summary: Paperclip updated with my experience from a hackathon.

entry.title: Inspiration for objWiki
entry.summary: Updated (added new links and introductions of interesting projects).

entry.title: SolarPi experiment 1: The PiJuice Fiasco
entry.summary: I wanted to create a home server that was capable of running on solar power. I failed. Mostly because I was too naive and believed that a specific product would solve this problem for me, and it didn’t. At least I can share the experience with you, so you don’t need to repeat my mistake.

entry.title: How to run your own LLM (GPT)
entry.summary: Some context about GPT & LLM (Large Language Models), how to run them on your own computer, and some examples how I use GPT4.

entry.title: SolarPi experiment 2: Finally something that works
entry.summary: After more than a year, I finally got to the point where I have more or less 24/7 solar-powered Raspberry Pi microserver.

entry.title: CaptchaCoin
entry.summary: An old concept about time-multiplexing specific assets in the form of a "cryptocurrency." Backported from depth of my personal wiki.

entry.title: HobbyCell
entry.summary: Startup idea for 3D printing-friendly electronic components which can be picked & placed by the 3D printer itself. Kinda like lego, but for electronics. Backported from depth of my personal wiki.

entry.title: Prusa MK4 upgrade
entry.summary: I've upgraded my Prusa MK3S+ 3D printer to MK4. This writeup contains the story of how it went and some observations about the improvements.

entry.title: Lessons learned from games
entry.summary: Some games make abstract engineering concepts feel physical. Belts and assemblers turn throughput into something you can see. Budgets turn refactoring into a trade-off you can’t ignore. Closed systems make early decisions echo much later. This post collects the few times that feeling survived the game and followed me back into my work.

entry.title: Unbounded battle in multiple dimensions
entry.summary: Creativity — the kind this essay is about — doesn't live inside one head. It emerges between minds as a response to a provocation: a move in a game no single player controls.
